q: Which company created ChatGPT?
explanation: ChatGPT was created by OpenAI; the others build Claude, Gemini, and Llama respectively.

q: Which company created the AI assistant 'Claude'?
explanation: Claude is built by Anthropic; OpenAI makes ChatGPT, Microsoft makes Copilot, Mistral makes Le Chat.

q: An AI 'chatbot' interacts with users primarily through:
options: Structured SQL queries typed by the user
explanation: Chatbots communicate via natural language; the distractors are unrelated AI techniques.

q: 'GitHub Copilot' is best described as an AI:
options: Coding assistant that suggests and completes code
explanation: GitHub Copilot helps developers write code; the others describe different AI applications.

q: 'Microsoft Copilot' was previously branded as:
explanation: Microsoft rebranded Bing Chat as Microsoft Copilot; Cortana was a separate older assistant.

q: Google's AI assistant 'Gemini' was formerly called:
explanation: Bard was rebranded to Gemini; LaMDA and PaLM were underlying Google models, Duplex was a calling feature.

q: In 'GPT', the letters stand for:
explanation: GPT = Generative Pre-trained Transformer, the architecture behind ChatGPT.

q: Meta's family of open-weight large language models is called:
explanation: Meta develops Llama; Gemini is Google's, Claude is Anthropic's, Phi is Microsoft's small-model family.

q: The technology powering assistants like ChatGPT and Claude is a:
explanation: Modern conversational assistants are built on LLMs; the others suit vision, classification, and sequences.

q: Microsoft 365 Copilot integrates AI assistance directly into:
options: The Windows kernel and device drivers
Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, and Teams
explanation: M365 Copilot embeds AI help across the Office productivity apps.

q: How does an AI 'agent' differ from a plain chatbot?
options: It can plan and take actions using tools to complete tasks
It runs only on specialised GPU hardware
It responds only with pre-written canned replies
explanation: Agents reason, use tools/APIs, and act toward goals — beyond simply chatting.

q: 'Function calling' (tool use) lets an AI assistant:
options: Increase its parameter count at runtime
Invoke external tools or APIs with structured arguments
explanation: Tool/function calling enables assistants to trigger external actions like search or code execution.

q: A 'prompt' given to an AI assistant is:
options: The dataset used to pre-train it
The metric used to evaluate it
The input instruction that guides its response
The number of layers in its network
explanation: A prompt is the input text that steers the assistant's output.

q: A 'hallucination' from an AI assistant refers to:
options: An image the model generates unprompted
A request the model refuses to answer
A confident but factually false or fabricated answer
explanation: Hallucinations are plausible-sounding but incorrect outputs.

q: The 'context window' of an assistant determines:
options: How fast it can generate each token
How large its training dataset was
How much text (tokens) it can consider at once
How many users can connect simultaneously
explanation: The context window is the maximum amount of text the model can attend to in one request.

q: 'RAG' (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) improves an assistant by:
options: Retrieving relevant documents to ground its answers
explanation: RAG fetches relevant external context so responses are grounded and up to date.

q: Which company develops the 'Gemini' family of models?
explanation: Gemini is developed by Google (Google DeepMind).

q: 'Mistral AI', known for open-weight models, is a company based in:
explanation: Mistral AI is a French company known for efficient open-weight LLMs.

q: Anthropic's Claude 3 model family included versions named:
explanation: Claude 3 shipped as Haiku (fastest), Sonnet (balanced), and Opus (most capable).

q: OpenAI's text-to-image generation model is called:
explanation: DALL-E generates images; Whisper does speech-to-text, Codex was for code, Sora for video.

q: OpenAI's speech-to-text (transcription) model is called:
explanation: Whisper is OpenAI's automatic speech-recognition model.

q: A 'system prompt' for an AI assistant typically:
options: Sets the assistant's role, behaviour, and rules
Selects which GPU the model runs on
explanation: The system prompt provides high-level instructions shaping the assistant's persona and constraints.

q: The 'temperature' setting in a generative assistant controls:
options: The size of the training set
The number of tool calls allowed
explanation: Higher temperature yields more varied output; lower makes it more deterministic.

q: 'Tokens', in the context of an LLM assistant, are:
options: Chunks of text (words or subwords) the model processes
explanation: Assistants read and generate text in units called tokens.

q: A 'multimodal' assistant is one that can:
options: Only respond in multiple human languages
Accept multiple input types, such as text and images
explanation: Multimodal assistants handle combinations of modalities like text, images, and audio.

q: 'GitHub Copilot' can be used inside which of the following?
options: Code editors such as VS Code and JetBrains IDEs
explanation: Copilot integrates into editors like VS Code, Visual Studio, JetBrains IDEs, and Neovim.

q: 'GitHub Copilot Chat' differs from inline completion because it:
options: Compiles and ships the final binary
Answers coding questions conversationally in the editor
explanation: Copilot Chat provides a conversational Q&A experience alongside inline code suggestions.

q: GitHub Copilot was originally powered by a model derived from:
options: OpenAI's Codex (a GPT model for code)
explanation: GitHub Copilot launched using OpenAI Codex, a GPT model specialised for code.

q: 'Copilot in Excel' is designed to help users:
options: Analyse data and generate formulas from natural-language requests
explanation: Copilot in Excel turns plain-language requests into analysis, formulas, and charts.

q: 'Copilot in PowerPoint' can:
options: Draft a slide deck from a document or prompt
Compress the file to a ZIP
explanation: Copilot in PowerPoint generates draft decks from prompts or existing documents.

q: 'Copilot in Outlook' commonly helps users by:
options: Summarising long email threads and drafting replies
Configuring the mail server's DNS records
explanation: Copilot in Outlook summarises threads and helps compose responses.

q: 'Copilot in Microsoft Teams' is typically used to:
options: Summarise meetings and surface action items
explanation: Teams Copilot recaps meetings, captures decisions, and lists follow-up actions.

q: 'Microsoft Security Copilot' is designed to assist:
options: Security analysts investigating and responding to threats
explanation: Security Copilot is a generative-AI assistant for SOC/security operations and incident response.

q: 'Microsoft Copilot Studio' is a tool for:
options: Building and customising your own copilots/agents
explanation: Copilot Studio is a low-code platform to create custom copilots and agents.

q: Within a Microsoft 365 tenant, Copilot 'grounds' its answers using:
options: Organisational data accessed via Microsoft Graph
explanation: M365 Copilot grounds responses in tenant data (emails, docs, chats) through Microsoft Graph.

q: 'Copilot in Windows' is best described as:
options: An AI assistant built into the Windows operating system
explanation: Copilot in Windows is an integrated AI assistant accessible from the desktop.

q: A key enterprise selling point of Microsoft 365 Copilot is that it:
options: Shares tenant data publicly to improve answers
Respects existing data permissions and tenant boundaries
Stores every prompt on the user's desktop
explanation: M365 Copilot honours a user's existing permissions and keeps data within the tenant boundary.

q: Which best distinguishes GitHub Copilot from Microsoft 365 Copilot?
options: They are identical products with different names
GitHub Copilot assists coding; M365 Copilot assists Office productivity
M365 Copilot only writes source code
explanation: GitHub Copilot targets developers in the IDE; M365 Copilot targets productivity in Office apps.

q: 'Copilot agents' (or extensions) allow a copilot to:
options: Connect to specific data sources or perform specialised tasks
explanation: Agents/extensions extend a copilot to particular knowledge sources or workflows.

q: 'Agentic AI' refers to systems that:
options: Only answer a single question then stop
Plan and execute multi-step tasks, often using tools
Never take actions in the world
explanation: Agentic systems chain reasoning with tool use to accomplish goals with autonomy.

q: A 'jailbreak' attempt on an AI assistant tries to:
options: Bypass its safety guardrails to elicit disallowed output
explanation: Jailbreaks craft inputs that make the assistant ignore its safety rules.

q: 'Prompt injection' is a security risk in which:
options: Malicious input overrides the assistant's intended instructions
explanation: Prompt injection hides instructions in content to hijack the assistant's behaviour.

q: 'Guardrails' around an AI assistant exist to:
options: Keep outputs safe and within policy
explanation: Guardrails filter or steer responses to meet safety and policy requirements.

q: 'Prompt engineering' is the practice of:
options: Crafting inputs to obtain better assistant outputs
explanation: Prompt engineering optimises how requests are phrased to guide the assistant.

q: 'Few-shot prompting' provides the assistant with:
options: A few worked examples inside the prompt
explanation: Few-shot prompting includes example demonstrations directly in the prompt.

q: 'Zero-shot' use of an assistant means:
options: Asking it to do a task with only instructions, no examples
Fine-tuning it on custom data first
explanation: Zero-shot relies on the model's pre-trained knowledge with instructions but no examples.

q: 'RLHF', widely used to align assistants like ChatGPT and Claude, stands for:
explanation: RLHF fine-tunes models using human preference signals to make them more helpful and safe.

q: A 'vector embedding' is used by assistants to:
options: Represent text meaning as vectors for semantic search
explanation: Embeddings map text to vectors so similar meanings sit close together, powering retrieval.

q: In a RAG-based assistant, a 'vector database' is used to:
options: Store embeddings and retrieve them by similarity
explanation: Vector databases index embeddings for fast nearest-neighbour retrieval of relevant context.

q: 'Fine-tuning' an assistant's underlying model means:
options: Further training it on task- or domain-specific data
explanation: Fine-tuning adapts a pre-trained model to a specific domain or task via additional training.

q: 'LoRA', often used to customise assistant models efficiently, is a form of:
explanation: LoRA trains small low-rank adapter matrices while freezing the base weights, saving compute.

q: 'Perplexity AI' is best described as an AI-powered:
options: Answer engine that cites its sources
explanation: Perplexity is an AI assistant that answers questions with linked citations.

q: 'Grounding' an assistant's answer means:
options: Shortening it to fit the screen
Backing it with reliable sources or retrieved context
explanation: Grounding ties responses to trustworthy sources to improve factual accuracy.

q: 'Chain-of-thought' prompting encourages an assistant to:
options: Answer in a single word only
Generate an image instead of text
Show intermediate reasoning steps before answering
explanation: Chain-of-thought elicits step-by-step reasoning, improving performance on complex tasks.

q: A 'reward model' in RLHF is trained to:
options: Score outputs by predicted human preference
explanation: The reward model predicts human preference scores that guide reinforcement learning.

q: Which statement correctly separates these Microsoft products?
options: Security Copilot aids security ops; GitHub Copilot aids coding
Security Copilot writes code; GitHub Copilot analyses malware
explanation: Security Copilot supports security analysts; GitHub Copilot supports developers writing code.

q: A practical reason to use RAG instead of fine-tuning for company knowledge is that RAG:
options: Permanently bakes facts into the weights
Lets you update the knowledge without retraining the model
Removes the need for any prompt
explanation: RAG keeps knowledge in an external store you can update anytime, avoiding costly retraining.

q: A shared limitation of assistants like ChatGPT, Claude, and Copilot is that they can:
options: Operate with no compute at all
Access any private system without permission
explanation: All current LLM-based assistants can hallucinate, so outputs should be verified.

q: 'Model Context Protocol'-style tool integrations let an assistant:
options: Connect to external tools and data sources in a standard way
explanation: Standardised tool/data connectors let agents access external systems consistently and securely.

q: When GitHub Copilot suggests code, the developer should:
options: Review and test it, since it may contain errors
Disable all testing to save time
Merge it to production without any review
Assume it is always optimal and secure
explanation: Copilot suggestions can be wrong or insecure, so human review and testing remain essential.
